A new roof is one of the most important home improvements you can make in Florida. With the state’s unique climate challenges—including intense sunlight, humidity, hurricane winds, and salt air—choosing the right roofing material and contractor is critical.
This guide covers everything you need to know about roof replacement costs in Florida for 2026, broken down by material type, location, and project specifics.
Average Cost to Replace a Roof in Florida The average cost to replace a roof in Florida ranges from $8,000 to $30,000+ for a typical 1,500-2,000 square foot home.
Material Cost per Square Total Cost (1,500 sq ft) Longevity Asphalt Shingles $4-6/sq ft $6,000-9,000 15-25 years Metal Roofing $8-15/sq ft $12,000-22,500 40-70+ years Tile Roofing $10-25/sq ft $15,000-37,500 50-100+ years Flat Roof (TPO/PVC) $5-8/sq ft $7,500-12,000 15-30 years Note: One “square” = 100 square feet of roof area.
Florida Roofing Materials Breakdown Asphalt Shingles Asphalt shingles remain the most popular choice due to their affordability and ease of installation.
Pros:
Lowest upfront cost Easy to repair Wide variety of colors and styles Good for steep-slope roofs Cons:
Shorter lifespan in Florida’s heat Vulnerable to high winds Can fade and curl over time Average Cost: $4,000-6,000 per square installed
Metal Roofing Metal roofing has become increasingly popular in Florida due to its durability and wind resistance.
Pros:
Excellent hurricane resistance (up to 140+ mph winds) Energy efficient (reflects heat) Lasts 40-70+ years Low maintenance Cons:
Higher initial cost Can be noisy during rain (unless properly insulated) Requires specialized installation Average Cost: $8,000-15,000 per square installed
Tile Roofing Clay and concrete tiles are iconic in Florida architecture and offer exceptional longevity.
Pros:
Extremely durable (50-100+ year lifespan) Fire resistant Excellent for high-wind areas Adds property value Cons:
Heaviest option (may need structural reinforcement) Highest upfront cost Fragile during installation Expensive to repair Average Cost: $10,000-25,000 per square installed
Flat Roof Options For commercial buildings or homes with flat sections, TPO and PVC membranes are popular.
Pros:
Good for flat or low-slope surfaces Seamless construction (no seams to leak) Energy efficient (white reflectivity) Good for HVAC equipment mounting Cons:
Requires proper drainage Vulnerable to punctures Shorter lifespan than pitched roofs Average Cost: $5,000-8,000 per square installed
Florida Location-Based Cost Variations Roofing costs vary significantly across Florida due to local market conditions, material availability, and hurricane risk standards.
South Florida (Miami, Fort Lauderdale, Palm Beach) South Florida has the highest roofing costs in the state due to:
High hurricane wind codes (must meet Miami-Dade County products) Import costs (most materials shipped from elsewhere) High demand ( frequent roof replacements after storms) Stricter building codes (more inspections, higher labor rates) Typical Range: $10,000-30,000+ for replacement
Central Florida (Orlando, Tampa, Jacksonville) Central Florida has moderate pricing with good material availability.
Typical Range: $8,000-25,000 for replacement
North Florida (Gainesville, Tallahassee) North Florida has lower costs closer to national averages.
Typical Range: $7,000-20,000 for replacement
Additional Cost Factors Roof Complexity Factor Cost Increase Multiple layers (tear-off required) +15-25% Complex design (valleys, dormers) +20-40% Steep slope (over 6:12) +25-50% Historic preservation requirements +30-60% Removal and Disposal Tear-off and disposal: $1,500-4,000 (depending on layers) Reroofing over existing (if allowed): Saves 20-30% Permits and Inspections Building permit: $500-1,500 (varies by county) Wind mitigation inspection: $100-250 (may qualify for insurance discount) Florida-Specific Considerations Hurricane Wind Ratings All roofing materials in Florida must meet specific wind speed ratings:
Material Minimum Wind Rating Asphalt Shingles 110 mph (most areas), 140+ mph (coastal) Metal Roofing 140-180+ mph Tile Roofing 140+ mph (properly installed) Insurance Discounts Many Florida insurance companies offer discounts for:
Impact-resistant roofing Wind mitigation features Newer roofs (0-10 years) Potential Savings: $200-500 per year on premiums
Getting Multiple Quotes We recommend getting at least 3 quotes from licensed, insured contractors.
Questions to ask:
Are you licensed and insured in Florida? Do you have experience with my roof type and local codes? What’s your warranty on labor and materials? Can you provide references from recent projects? How do you handle storm damage claims? Conclusion Replacing your roof in Florida is a significant investment, but the right choice will protect your home for decades. Consider your budget, how long you plan to stay in the home, and the specific climate challenges in your area.
Quick Tips:
Get multiple quotes (at least 3) Check contractor licenses at MYFLORIDA.com Ask about warranty coverage Consider long-term maintenance costs Ask about insurance discounts for new roofs Last Updated: April 2026 | For the most current pricing, always get personalized quotes from local contractors.

Quick Answer The average cost to apply roof coating in Florida in 2026 ranges from $3,500 to $12,000 for a typical home. Most Florida homeowners pay around $7,500 depending on size, materials, and local permit requirements.
This cost covers surface preparation, material application, and warranty registration. Prices fluctuate based on roof type (tile, metal, or shingle), accessibility, and whether the project falls under High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ) mandates in South Florida.
Overview Applying a reflective roof coating system is one of the most cost-effective ways to extend the life of an aging roof in the Sunshine State. Unlike a full tear-off and replacement, which can cost $20,000 to $50,000 depending on the home size, roof coating seals existing cracks, reflects harmful UV rays, and restores waterproofing integrity. In Florida, this is not just about aesthetics; it is a critical defense mechanism against the state’s aggressive climate.
Florida’s environment is uniquely punishing on roofing materials. Intense UV exposure degrades asphalt shingles and concrete tiles rapidly, causing them to become brittle and lose their granular protection. High humidity promotes mold and algae growth, which can eat away at the substrate over time. Furthermore, salt air in coastal regions accelerates corrosion on metal roofing components. A high-quality elastomeric roof coating acts as a protective skin, reflecting up to 85% of solar heat and reducing interior cooling costs by 10-20% during the summer months.
The cost variation in 2026 is driven primarily by the type of coating material selected. Acrylic coatings are water-based and cost-effective but require dry weather for application. Silicone coatings offer superior water resistance and durability but come at a premium price. Additionally, the condition of the existing roof dictates the prep work required. If the substrate requires extensive repair before coating can be applied, labor costs will rise significantly.
Crucially, Florida homeowners must adhere to the Florida Building Code (FBC). The 8th Edition of the FBC mandates specific wind uplift resistance standards for roofing systems. A roof coating application often counts as a “re-roofing” event, requiring a permit and inspection to ensure the system meets the wind load requirements for the specific county. Ignoring these regulations can void insurance coverage and lead to fines. Therefore, budgeting for proper permits and licensed labor is not optional; it is essential for compliance and long-term protection.
Average Cost Breakdown Project Type Low Estimate High Estimate Average Tile Roof Coating $4,500 $11,000 $7,750 Metal Roof Coating $3,500 $9,500 $6,500 Shingle/Asphalt Coating $3,500 $8,000 $5,750 Concrete/Flat Roof Coating $5,000 $13,000 $9,000 All prices based on 2026 Florida market rates. Labor costs vary by county.
What’s Included in These Estimates When you receive a quote for roof coating in Florida, a professional contractor should provide a transparent breakdown. The estimates above generally include:
Initial Inspection: A thorough assessment of the roof deck, flashing, and existing coating condition. Surface Preparation: High-pressure washing to remove dirt, algae, and loose debris. Repairs: Patching small cracks, resealing flashing, and replacing broken tiles if necessary. Primer Application: Applying a bonding agent to ensure the coating adheres properly to the substrate. Material Application: Typically two coats of elastomeric coating are applied for optimal coverage and durability. Cleanup: Removal of all debris and protection of landscaping, gutters, and windows. Warranty Registration: Filing the warranty with the manufacturer for coverage periods ranging from 5 to 10 years. Exclusions: These estimates typically do not include structural repairs (such as replacing decking or rafters), skylight removal/reinstallation, or major flashing replacements that require specialized metalwork. If your roof requires significant structural work, a separate line item will be added to the contract.
Cost Factors in Florida Florida Building Code Requirements Florida follows the Florida Building Code (FBC) 8th Edition, which sets strict standards for all roofing work. For roof coatings, Chapter 15 (Roof Assemblies and Rooftop Structures) dictates the requirements. One of the most critical aspects is wind resistance. The coating system must be tested to ensure it does not peel or delaminate under high wind speeds.
High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ): Properties in Miami-Dade and Broward counties fall under HVHZ rules, which require specific product approvals. Coating systems used in these zones must have passed Miami-Dade County TAS 100 (Impact Resistance) and TAS 125 (Wind Resistance) testing. This adds $1,500–$3,000 to total costs because specialized HVHZ-certified materials are more expensive and require certified applicators who understand the specific application thickness requirements mandated by the county building department.
Location Matters Geography plays a massive role in pricing due to labor rates, permit fees, and environmental challenges.
County / City Estimated Cost Range Notes Miami-Dade $6,000 – $14,000 HVHZ requirements apply; higher permit fees Broward County $5,500 – $13,000 HVHZ requirements apply; coastal wind load Palm Beach $5,000 – $12,000 Coastal wind zone; salt air considerations Tampa / Hillsborough $4,000 – $11,000 Wind Zone III; high humidity impact Orlando / Orange County $3,500 – $9,500 Inland, lower wind requirements Jacksonville / Duval $4,500 – $11,500 Mixed coastal/inland; humidity concerns Other Cost Factors Beyond location and code requirements, several specific variables will influence your final bill in 2026.
Roof Accessibility and Pitch: Steep roofs or homes with multiple stories require specialized safety equipment and scaffolding. If the roof is difficult to access, labor costs can increase by 20-30%. Coating Material Grade: Basic acrylics are cheaper but may need re-application in 3-5 years. High-grade silicone or urethane coatings cost more upfront but can last 10-15 years. Existing Roof Condition: If the roof has extensive moss, algae, or loose tiles, the prep time increases. A roof that is “clean and ready” costs less to coat than one that requires deep cleaning and minor repairs. Seasonal Demand: While Florida doesn’t have a traditional “winter,” the dry season (November to April) is peak season for contractors. Scheduling during the wet season (June to October) might yield a 10% discount, but rain delays are a risk. Contractor Experience: A licensed, insured specialist with a track record of coating applications will charge more than a general handyman. However, the risk of failure is significantly lower with a specialist, protecting your investment. How to Save Money Bundle with Other Projects: If you are planning to replace gutters, repair skylights, or clean solar panels, combine these tasks with the roof coating. Contractors often offer a discounted labor rate when multiple services are performed on the same day. Time It Right: Schedule the work for the shoulder season (late May or early October). Contractors are busier in the winter and spring, so demand is lower in late summer, potentially leading to better pricing. My Safe Florida Home Program: Check if you qualify for the My Safe Florida Home program. While primarily for wind mitigation (like roof straps), some grants and tax incentives for energy-efficient improvements may overlap. Applying for a wind mitigation inspection can sometimes lead to premium reductions that offset the project cost over time. DIY Prep: If you have the ability, you can handle the landscaping protection and move vehicles away from the driveway yourself. This saves the contractor a few hours of labor, which can be passed on as a small discount. Get multiple quotes: Florida law requires licensed contractors. Use myfloridalicense.com to verify. Getting 3+ quotes typically saves 10–20% as you can compare pricing and scope. Warning Signs to Watch For ⚠️ These are red flags when hiring a contractor in Florida.
Unlicensed Work: If a contractor claims they don’t need a license for “small jobs,” that is false. Roofing work in Florida always requires a state license. Verify their status immediately. No Permit Pull: If they say you don’t need a permit, run. In Florida, roof coating is generally considered a re-roofing activity requiring a permit. Skipping this risks fines and insurance denial. Cash-Only Discounts: A request for payment in cash to avoid taxes or insurance tracking is a major warning sign. It often indicates they are operating off the books and may not carry proper insurance. Upfront Payment Demands: Florida law prohibits collecting more than 10% or $1,000 upfront for residential projects. If they demand 50% before starting, walk away. Storm chaser scams: After hurricanes, out-of-state contractors flood Florida. Verify any contractor’s Florida license at myfloridalicense.com before signing anything. How to Hire a Contractor in Florida Step 1: Verify the License All Florida contractors must hold a state license. Check at myfloridalicense.com. For roofing work, look for a Certified Roofing Contractor license. This ensures they are qualified to work anywhere in the state. If they are “Registered,” they can only work in specific counties. Ensure the license is active and in good standing.
Step 2: Get the Permit In Florida, most home improvement projects require a permit. Your contractor should pull it — if they say you don’t need one, that’s a red flag. Permit fees typically run $150–$500 depending on county. The permit ensures the work is inspected for code compliance, which protects you if the roof fails later.
Step 3: Get 3+ Quotes Prices vary 20–40% between contractors. Get quotes from at least three. Each quote should include: detailed square footage calculations, brand and type of coating specified, number of coats to be applied, warranty terms, start and completion dates, and a line-item breakdown of labor and materials.
Step 4: Check Insurance Contractor must carry general liability AND workers’ comp. Ask for certificates of insurance, not just verbal confirmation. If a worker gets hurt on your property and they don’t have workers’ comp, you could be liable for their medical bills. Verify the policy is current.
Step 5: Understand Your Contract What should be in a proper contract. Payment schedule — never pay more than 10% or $1,000 upfront per Florida law. The contract must include the total price, the scope of work (e.g., “clean, prime, and coat all accessible areas”), the specific product being used (e.g., “Acrylic Elastomeric”), and the warranty duration. It should also state that the contractor is responsible for obtaining all necessary permits and passing final inspections.
FAQ How long does roof coating take in Florida? For a typical single-family home (2,000 sq ft), the process takes 2 to 4 days. Day 1 is for inspection and power washing. Day 2 is for repairs and priming. Days 3 and 4 are for applying the coats. However, weather delays due to rain or high humidity are common in Florida, so always add a buffer of 3-5 days to the timeline.
Do I need a permit for roof coating in Florida? Yes, in most counties, applying a coating system is considered a “re-roofing” activity and requires a building permit. This is especially true in Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach counties due to wind code requirements. The contractor usually pulls this permit as part of the job cost.
Can I do roof coating myself in Florida? Technically, you can, but it is not recommended for most homeowners. The surface prep is critical; if you don’t clean the roof properly, the coating will peel. Additionally, working on a roof in Florida’s heat and humidity poses significant safety risks. Furthermore, DIY work voids most manufacturer warranties, which require certified installation.
What’s the best time of year to apply coating in Florida? The dry season (November through April) is ideal. You need at least 24-48 hours of dry weather after application for the coating to cure. Avoid the peak of hurricane season (August-September) if possible, as sudden rainstorms can ruin the curing process.
Will this affect my homeowner’s insurance in Florida? Yes, positively. A new roof coating system can improve your home’s wind mitigation rating. Many Florida insurers offer discounts for roofs that are less than 10 years old or have been treated with approved reflective materials. Always provide the warranty and permit documentation to your insurance agent.
How do I find a licensed roofing contractor in Florida? Search the database at myfloridalicense.com for “Certified Roofing Contractor.” Look for contractors with at least 5 years of experience in coating specifically, as not all roofers are experts in coating application. Check reviews on Google and the BBB for recent feedback regarding warranty claims.
Bottom Line Florida homeowners should budget $3,500–$12,000 for professional roof coating in 2026.
The biggest cost drivers are HVHZ compliance in South Florida and the condition of the existing roof surface. To get the best price: verify your contractor’s Florida license, pull the permit yourself if needed, and get at least 3 quotes before signing anything.
For My Safe Florida Home grants and resources relevant to this project type, visit safefloridahome.org.
Last updated: 2026-01-15 | Prices based on 2026 Florida contractor market data. Always get personalized quotes — costs vary significantly by county, home size, and project scope.

Quick Answer The average cost to roof coating in Fort Lauderdale in 2026 ranges from $5,000 to $14,000 for a typical single-family home. Most Fort Lauderdale homeowners pay around $9,500 depending on size, materials, and local permit requirements.
Overview Roof coating is one of the most cost-effective ways to extend the life of an existing roof in South Florida, but understanding the specific costs and regulatory landscape in 2026 is crucial for Fort Lauderdale homeowners. Unlike other regions, Florida’s unique climate presents aggressive challenges to roofing systems. The combination of intense UV radiation, high humidity, salt air exposure from the Atlantic Ocean, and the threat of hurricanes creates a demanding environment for any building envelope.
Applying a roof coating acts as a protective shield, reflecting harmful UV rays and sealing minor cracks before they lead to major leaks. For Fort Lauderdale properties, this is not just about aesthetics; it is a strategic maintenance move required to maintain insurance compliance and building code adherence. The state’s building codes have tightened significantly with the adoption of the Florida Building Code (FBC) 8th Edition, which now mandates stricter standards for roof reflectivity and wind uplift resistance.
The cost of roof coating varies based on the type of material selected—acrylic, silicone, or polyurethane—and the condition of the existing substrate. While a new roof installation can cost upwards of $20,000 to $40,000 for an average home, coating is a fraction of that price. However, the 2026 market in Broward County reflects rising labor costs and material inflation. Homeowners must also account for the necessary surface preparation, which is often the most labor-intensive part of the job. If the existing roof is damaged, coatings will not adhere properly, requiring repairs before the coating process can begin. This guide breaks down the real costs, legal requirements, and hidden fees associated with roof coating projects in Fort Lauderdale for 2026.
Average Cost Breakdown Understanding the price range is the first step in budgeting for a roof coating project. The following table outlines the estimated costs based on a standard 2,000 square foot roof area, which is typical for a single-family home in Fort Lauderdale. Prices fluctuate based on roof pitch, accessibility, and the specific coating system chosen.
Project Type Low Estimate High Estimate Average Acrylic Roof Coating (2,000 sq ft) $4,500 $9,000 $6,750 Silicone Roof Coating (2,000 sq ft) $8,000 $14,000 $11,000 Polyurethane Roof Coating (2,000 sq ft) $10,000 $18,000 $14,000 All prices based on 2026 Florida market rates. Labor costs vary by county. Estimates include surface prep, primer, and two coats of material.
What’s Included in These Estimates When you receive a quote from a Fort Lauderdale contractor, the price should cover specific deliverables. If these items are missing, you may be looking at hidden costs later.
Surface Preparation: Pressure washing, removing debris, and repairing small cracks or blisters. Primer Application: Essential for adhesion, especially on older asphalt or concrete roofs. Reinforcement Fabric: Mesh applied over seams and flashings to prevent cracking. Base and Top Coats: Typically a two-coat system for warranty validity. Cleanup: Removal of all debris and protection of landscaping during the job. Warranty Documentation: Manufacturer warranty cards and contractor workmanship warranty. Exclusions: Major structural repairs, replacing flashing, or fixing extensive water damage to the decking usually require a separate contract and additional budget.
Cost Factors in Florida Florida Building Code Requirements Florida follows the Florida Building Code (FBC) 8th Edition, which was adopted to enhance resilience against natural disasters. For roof coating projects, this code dictates specific performance standards that directly influence material selection and pricing. The FBC requires that coatings used in residential applications must meet specific fire resistance and wind uplift ratings.
High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ): Fort Lauderdale is located in Broward County, which falls under the High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ). This designation imposes stricter requirements than standard wind zones. Properties in this zone must use materials that have been tested and approved for high-wind environments. While HVHZ rules are most strictly associated with Miami-Dade, Broward County enforces similar wind uplift standards for coastal communities. This adds $500–$1,500 to total costs because approved HVHZ-rated materials are more expensive than standard commercial coatings. Additionally, the coating system must be tested to withstand wind uplift pressures of 140 mph or higher, depending on the specific building height and location within the zone.
Testing Standards: To comply with FBC 8th Edition, coating systems must pass rigorous testing beyond basic fire ratings. Contractors must verify that the products meet ASTM E108 (Standard Test Methods for Fire Tests of Roof Coverings) and UL 790 (Standard Test Methods for Fire Tests of Roof Coverings). Furthermore, elastomeric coatings must meet ASTM D3924 (Standard Specification for Elastomeric Roof Coatings) to ensure they can expand and contract with the roof substrate without cracking. Some premium systems also require ASTM D522 (Flexibility) testing to ensure the material remains pliable in Florida’s heat. These certifications add a premium to the material cost but are non-negotiable for code compliance.
Location Matters Costs vary significantly across Florida due to permit fees, labor availability, and specific local wind zone requirements.
County / City Estimated Cost Range Notes Miami-Dade $5,500 – $15,000 Strict HVHZ requirements apply Broward County $5,000 – $14,000 HVHZ requirements apply (Fort Lauderdale) Palm Beach $4,800 – $13,500 Coastal wind zone, similar to Broward Tampa / Hillsborough $4,500 – $12,000 Wind Zone III, no HVHZ Orlando / Orange County $4,000 – $11,000 Inland, lower wind requirements Jacksonville / Duval $4,200 – $12,500 Mixed coastal/inland Other Cost Factors Beyond location and code requirements, several other variables impact the final price of a roof coating project in 2026.
Roof Pitch and Accessibility: Steeper roofs require more safety equipment and labor time. A flat or low-slope roof is significantly cheaper to coat than a steep gable roof. Material Grade: Basic acrylic coatings cost less but have a shorter lifespan (5–7 years). High-grade silicone coatings cost more but last 10–15 years and offer better waterproofing. Roof Condition: If the existing roof has extensive ponding water or large cracks, extensive repairs are needed before coating. This can add $2,000+ to the project. Seasonal Demand: Roofing is a year-round industry in Florida, but demand spikes after hurricane season (October–November). Scheduling in the dry season (December–April) may secure better pricing. Contractor Experience: Specialized coating contractors charge a premium but offer better warranties. General roofers may not have the specific expertise for coating systems. How to Save Money Roof coating is already a budget-friendly alternative to full replacement, but there are ways to optimize the cost without sacrificing quality.
Schedule During the Off-Season — Roofing contractors are often busiest from May to October due to storm preparation and post-storm repairs. Scheduling your coating project between November and March can sometimes secure a 10–15% discount due to lower demand. Bundle Maintenance Tasks — If you have gutters, skylights, or vents that need attention, combine these repairs with the coating project. Contractors are more likely to offer a discount on labor when multiple tasks are performed in a single day. Utilize My Safe Florida Home — Check if your roof coating qualifies for the My Safe Florida Home program. While primarily for roof replacements, some energy-efficient reflective coatings may qualify for grants or insurance premium reductions. Visit mysafefloridahome.org to check eligibility. Choose Acrylic Over Silicone for Sun Exposure — If your roof is primarily exposed to sun rather than heavy ponding water, high-quality acrylic coatings are significantly cheaper than silicone and still provide excellent UV reflection and longevity. Get Multiple Quotes — Florida law requires licensed contractors. Use myfloridalicense.com to verify. Getting 3+ quotes typically saves 10–20%. Prices vary 20–40% between contractors due to overhead and material sourcing. Warning Signs to Watch For ⚠️ These are red flags when hiring a contractor in Florida.
[No License Number Posted]: The contractor refuses to show their license number or the number on their truck does not match the state database. This is illegal in Florida.
[Demanding Full Cash Upfront]: Florida law caps upfront payments at 10% or $1,000, whichever is less. Any demand for full payment before work begins is a major scam indicator.
[Refusal to Pull Permits]: In Broward County, most roof coatings require a permit if the work involves structural changes or significant surface prep. If they say you don’t need one, they may be cutting corners or operating illegally.
[No Written Warranty]: A verbal warranty is worthless. If the manufacturer or contractor does not provide a written warranty document included in the contract, walk away.
Storm Chaser Scams: After hurricanes, out-of-state contractors flood Florida. Verify any contractor’s Florida license at myfloridalicense.com before signing anything.
How to Hire a Contractor in Florida Step 1: Verify the License All Florida contractors must hold a state license. Check at myfloridalicense.com. For roofing work, look for a Roofing Contractor (CGC) or Building Contractor (CB) license with a “R” designation. Ensure the license is active and in good standing.
Step 2: Get the Permit In Florida, most home improvement projects require a permit. Your contractor should pull it — if they say you don’t need one, that’s a red flag. Permit fees typically run $200–$500 depending on county. Broward County requires inspection after the coating is applied to verify fire and wind ratings.
Step 3: Get 3+ Quotes Prices vary 20–40% between contractors. Get quotes from at least three. Each quote should include:
Total square footage of the roof. Specific brand and type of coating material. Number of coats to be applied. Surface preparation details (washing, patching). Warranty terms (manufacturer and workmanship). Timeline for completion. Total cost including permits and cleanup. Step 4: Check Insurance Contractor must carry general liability AND workers’ comp. Ask for certificates of insurance, not just verbal confirmation. This protects you from liability if a worker is injured on your property.
Step 5: Understand Your Contract Your contract must be in writing and include the start date, completion date, payment schedule, and a detailed description of the work. Payment schedule — never pay more than 10% or $1,000 upfront per Florida law. Milestone payments are standard (e.g., 30% start, 40% material delivery, 30% completion). Ensure the contract specifies that the contractor is responsible for obtaining all necessary permits and passing inspections.
FAQ How long does roof coating take in Florida? For a typical 2,000 sq ft roof, the process usually takes 2–3 days. Day 1 is for surface preparation and repairs. Day 2 is for priming and the first coat. Day 3 is for the second coat. However, curing time depends on weather; humidity and rain can delay the schedule.
Do I need a permit for roof coating in Florida? Yes, in most Florida counties, including Broward, a permit is required for roof coating if it involves significant surface preparation or changes to the roof system. The permit ensures the coating meets FBC 8th Edition fire and wind standards.
Can I do roof coating myself in Florida? No, DIY roof coating is not recommended. Florida law requires licensed professionals for most roofing work. Additionally, improper application can void manufacturer warranties and lead to water damage claims.
What’s the best time of year to coat a roof in Florida? The dry season (December to April) is ideal. High humidity and rain in the summer (May to November) can prevent proper curing. Avoid scheduling during hurricane season if possible to prevent work stoppages.
Will this affect my homeowner’s insurance in Florida? Yes, a reflective roof coating can sometimes lower your premium by improving energy efficiency and wind resistance. However, you must notify your insurer and provide proof of the coating material’s wind rating to get credit.
How do I find a licensed roofing contractor in Florida? Search for contractors on myfloridalicense.com. You can also check the National Roofing Contractors Association (NRCA) directory or the Better Business Bureau (BBB) for local reviews and complaint history.
Bottom Line Fort Lauderdale homeowners should budget $5,000–$14,000 for a professional roof coating project in 2026.
Fort Lauderdale homeowners should budget $5,000–$14,000 for this project in 2026. The biggest cost drivers are HVHZ compliance, roof condition, and material selection. Quick Answer The average cost to coat your roof in Miami in 2026 ranges from $2,500 to $6,500 for a typical home. Most Miami homeowners pay around $4,500 depending on size, materials, and local permit requirements.
Overview Roof coating is a maintenance solution designed to extend the lifespan of your existing roofing system by adding a protective layer over the current surface. In Miami, this process is critical due to the intense UV exposure, high humidity, and salt air that can degrade roofing materials faster than in other regions. A high-quality elastomeric or silicone coating can reflect heat, reduce energy bills, and seal minor cracks that lead to leaks.
Florida’s unique climate makes roof coating particularly valuable compared to other parts of the country. The state’s building codes and environmental factors require materials that can withstand thermal expansion, heavy rain, and hurricane-force winds. Unlike standard glass, these doors feature laminated glass layers bonded with a polyvinyl butyral (PVB) interlayer that holds the glass together even when shattered. This structural integrity prevents the door from breaching, which is crucial for maintaining the building envelope’s pressure rating during a storm.
Florida’s unique climate presents specific challenges that standard doors cannot withstand. The state experiences high humidity, intense UV exposure, and salt air, particularly in coastal regions like Miami-Dade, Broward, and Palm Beach. These environmental factors accelerate corrosion on metal frames and degrade sealants on standard units. Impact doors are specifically treated and constructed with materials like aluminum, vinyl, or fiberglass that resist corrosion and thermal expansion, ensuring longevity in the harsh Florida environment. Furthermore, the Florida Building Code (FBC) mandates specific performance standards for these products to ensure they meet local safety requirements.
The cost variation is driven by several factors, including the type of door (entryway vs. sliding patio), the wind load zone of the property, and the complexity of the installation. In High-Velocity Hurricane Zones (HVHZ), such as Miami-Dade and Broward counties, regulations are stricter, often requiring specific testing certifications like Miami-Dade NOA (Notice of Acceptance). These stricter requirements can increase the upfront cost but provide higher safety ratings. Additionally, labor costs vary significantly between inland counties like Orlando and coastal hubs like Tampa, where specialized hurricane protection contractors command higher rates due to demand and expertise. Understanding these variables is essential for accurate budgeting.

Overview Installing impact-resistant windows is one of the most critical home improvement projects for homeowners in South Florida, particularly in Broward County. These specialized windows are engineered to withstand high-velocity wind pressures and flying debris, serving as a primary defense against hurricane damage. Beyond hurricane protection, impact windows offer significant benefits including noise reduction, UV protection to prevent furniture fading, and enhanced security against break-ins.
The history of impact windows in Florida is directly tied to the devastation caused by Hurricane Andrew in 1992. Before Andrew, building codes in South Florida were relatively lenient regarding window strength. Following the storm, which caused billions in damages due to window failures and wind-driven rain, the state implemented stricter regulations. By 1994, Miami-Dade and Broward counties began requiring impact-resistant glazing for new construction and major renovations in coastal zones. Over the last three decades, these requirements have evolved into the stringent High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ) standards enforced today under the Florida Building Code (FBC).
In 2026, the demand for impact windows remains high due to rising insurance premiums and increasing storm intensity. Florida’s unique climate presents specific challenges: intense humidity can warp frames, UV exposure degrades sealants, and salt air accelerates corrosion on metal components. The Florida Building Code 8th Edition mandates that all windows in coastal zones meet specific wind load ratings, typically 140 mph or higher depending on the building’s height and location. Failure to comply with these codes not only risks structural safety during a storm but can also lead to insurance claim denials. Understanding the cost drivers, permit processes, and grant opportunities is essential for Broward County residents planning this investment.
Average Cost Breakdown The total cost of installing impact windows varies widely based on the number of openings, frame material, and glass specifications. The table below outlines typical price ranges for a standard Broward County home in 2026.

What’s Included in These Estimates When reviewing contractor quotes for impact windows in Broward County, it is crucial to understand what is included in the price to avoid hidden fees.
Included: Professional measurement, removal and disposal of old windows, new impact-rated frames, laminated glass, installation labor, and basic caulking/weatherproofing. Excluded: Structural repairs to the framing (if rot is found), painting or staining of frames, window treatments (blinds/shades), permit fees, and electrical upgrades if new wiring is required for integrated screens or shades. Warranty: Most reputable contractors include a 10-year labor warranty and a manufacturer warranty on the glass (often 20 years) for delamination or seal failure. Cleanup: Standard installation includes sweeping and removal of construction debris, but deep cleaning of the home interior is typically excluded. Cost Factors in Florida Several variables influence the final price of an impact window project in 2026. While the base cost of the product is significant, local regulations and site conditions can alter the budget substantially.
Florida Building Code Requirements Florida follows the Florida Building Code (FBC) 8th Edition, which dictates strict performance standards for building components. For impact windows, the code requires testing for large missile impact (a 2x4 piece of lumber fired at the window) and cyclic pressure testing to simulate hurricane winds.
High-Velocity Hurricane Zone (HVHZ): Properties in Miami-Dade and Broward counties fall under HVHZ rules. This is the most stringent building zone in the state. Windows installed in these zones must meet specific air infiltration and water penetration ratings that exceed standard Florida requirements. This adds approximately $1,500–$3,000 to the total project cost compared to windows installed in inland Florida counties. Additionally, the installation process must be certified by a licensed engineer or the county building department to ensure the anchoring system meets the wind load requirements.

Frame Material: Aluminum is the standard for impact windows in Florida due to its strength and resistance to salt air corrosion. Tear-Off vs. Overlay: If you are replacing existing windows, contractors can sometimes install the new unit over the old frame (overlay), saving on labor and disposal costs. However, if the existing wood frame is rotted or damaged, a full tear-off is required, which adds $500–$2,000 to the project. Seasonality: Demand for window contractors peaks after hurricane season (October) and during spring construction seasons. Scheduling your project during the off-season (winter, January–March) can sometimes secure a 5–10% discount on labor rates. Contractor Experience: Specialized impact window installers often charge more than general handymen, but this is necessary in Broward County. Improper installation voids the warranty and compromises wind resistance. How to Save Money While impact windows are a significant investment, there are legitimate ways to reduce the cost without compromising safety or code compliance.
Bundle with Roofing: Many contractors offer discounts if you replace your roof and windows in the same project. Apply for Rebates: Check for utility rebates from Florida Power & Light (FPL) or other local providers. Energy-efficient impact windows with Low-E coatings often qualify for rebates ranging from $100 to $500 depending on the energy savings rating. Utilize My Safe Florida Home: If you are eligible, the My Safe Florida Home program offers grants specifically for mitigation improvements. Compare this with home equity lines of credit (HELOC), which may offer lower long-term rates but require home equity. Get multiple quotes: Florida law requires licensed contractors. How to Hire a Contractor in Florida Hiring the right professional is as important as the product itself. Improper installation can lead to leaks, wind damage, and voided warranties.

For hurricane-protection work, look for a Certified Roofing Contractor or General Contractor license with the appropriate classification. Ensure the license is active and in good standing with no disciplinary history.
Step 2: Get the Permit In Florida, most home improvement projects require a permit. Your contractor should pull it — if they say you don’t need one, that’s a red flag. Permit fees typically run $200–$500 depending on county and valuation. In Broward, the permit ensures the installation meets wind load codes.
Step 3: Get 3+ Quotes Prices vary 20–40% between contractors. Get quotes from at least three. Each quote should include:
Detailed scope of work (number of windows, sizes). Specific product models (manufacturer, glass type, frame color). Timeline for completion. Warranty terms (labor and manufacturer). Total cost including taxes and permits. Step 4: Check Insurance Contractor must carry general liability AND workers’ comp. Ask for certificates of insurance, not just verbal confirmation. If a worker gets hurt on your property and the contractor lacks insurance, you could be liable for medical bills.
Step 5: Understand Your Contract Florida law has specific requirements for home improvement contracts. It must include the contractor’s license number, a detailed description of the work, a payment schedule, and a right of rescission (3-day cancellation window). My Safe Florida Home Grant Inspection Process The My Safe Florida Home Program is a critical resource for homeowners looking to reduce insurance premiums and protect their homes. If you plan to install impact windows as part of this program, there is a specific inspection process you must follow.

This inspector will evaluate your home’s current mitigation features. They will check your roof, garage doors, and existing windows. If your home does not meet the minimum mitigation requirements, you may be ineligible for the grant. This inspection usually costs between $200 and $350 but is often reimbursable if you proceed with the grant.
Grant Application: Once the inspection confirms eligibility, you apply for the grant. The grant can cover up to $10,000 for mitigation improvements, which includes window upgrades. The application requires proof of insurance and documentation from the inspection.
Post-Installation Verification: After the impact windows are installed, a second inspection is required to verify the work was completed according to code and manufacturer specifications. The inspector will check for proper anchoring, seal integrity, and HVHZ compliance. You must submit the inspection report and invoices to the program administrator to receive the grant funds. This process ensures that the money is used for actual safety improvements and not substandard work.
Timeline: The entire grant process typically takes 4–6 weeks from the initial inspection to fund disbursement. It is important to factor this timeline into your construction schedule. Contractors often require payment upon completion, so you may need to cover the cost upfront and wait for the grant reimbursement.
FAQ How long does impact window installation take in Florida? A typical Broward County home with 10–15 windows takes 3–5 days to install. Custom orders can add 4–6 weeks for manufacturing. The actual installation on-site is usually completed in 2–3 days, with 1 day for prep and cleanup.
Do I need a permit for impact windows in Florida? Yes, almost always. In Broward County, replacing windows requires a building permit to ensure wind load compliance. Permit fees vary by city. For example, Fort Lauderdale typically charges around $350 for a residential window permit, while Hollywood may charge between $250 and $450 depending on the valuation of the project.
Can I do impact window installation myself in Florida? No. Florida law requires a licensed contractor to pull permits for structural and weatherproofing work. DIY installation is not only illegal for permit purposes but also voids the manufacturer’s warranty. Impact windows must be installed with specific sealants and anchoring that require professional certification.
What’s the best time of year to install windows in Florida? The best time is typically late winter or early spring (February–April). This avoids the peak hurricane season and the summer heat, which can affect curing sealants. However, contractors are busiest during these months, so booking 2–3 months in advance is recommended.
Will this affect my homeowner’s insurance in Florida? Yes, positively. Installing impact windows is one of the most effective ways to lower windstorm premiums. Many insurers offer discounts ranging from 15% to 45% for impact-rated glazing. However, you must notify your insurance company and provide the certification of installation to apply the credit.
How do insurance claims work for wind damage after installation? If your windows are damaged during a storm, the insurance company will inspect them. If they were properly installed and certified, the claim should be covered. If the installation was unpermitted or non-compliant, the claim may be denied. Always keep your permit and certification documents with your insurance files.
How do I find a licensed hurricane-protection contractor in Florida? Verify licenses at myfloridalicense.com. Look for contractors with “Wind Mitigation” or “Roofing” classifications. Check the BBB for complaints and ask for references from local Broward homeowners.
How long does the My Safe Florida Home grant inspection take? The inspection itself takes about 1–2 hours on-site. However, the scheduling and report processing can take 1–3 weeks. The post-installation verification is usually faster, often completed within 5 business days of the request.
What happens if the inspection fails for the grant? If the pre-installation inspection fails, you are not eligible for the grant until the required upgrades are made. This might mean reinforcing the roof or garage door before the windows can be funded. You would then need to reschedule the inspection.
Can I claim tax credits alongside the grant? Federal tax credits for energy-efficient windows (IRA 25C) may apply if the windows meet specific energy performance criteria. The My Safe Florida Home grant is a rebate, not a loan, so it does not affect tax liability. Consult a tax professional to see if you can stack both incentives.
Bottom Line Impact windows are a necessary investment for Broward County homeowners facing 2026 insurance rates and storm risks.
Broward homeowners should budget $15,000–$65,000 for this project in 2026. The biggest cost drivers are the HVHZ compliance requirements, the number of windows installed, and whether structural repairs are needed during the tear-off. To get the best price: verify your contractor’s Florida license, pull the permit yourself if needed, and get at least 3 quotes before signing anything.
For My Safe Florida Home grants, visit My Safe Florida Home.

Navigating Palm Beach Permits and HOAs Palm Beach County Permit Process In Palm Beach County, installing impact windows is considered a structural alteration. You cannot skip the permit process, even for replacements.
Application Submission: Submit plans showing the window schedule and product approval numbers. Plan Review: The building department reviews for code compliance. This typically takes 10–14 business days. Permit Fee: Fees are calculated based on the total project value. Expect to pay $500 to $2,500 in permit fees alone for a full home retrofit. Inspections: A rough inspection occurs after framing is done, and a final inspection happens once the windows are installed and sealed. Common Pitfall: Many homeowners try to avoid permits to save money. This is illegal and can lead to fines of up to $5,000 per violation, as well as issues when selling the home.
HOA Approval Nuances in Palm Beach Palm Beach is unique because it contains several incorporated towns (Palm Beach, Palm Beach Shores, Palm Beach Gardens, etc.) and numerous private HOAs.
Aesthetic Review: Most HOAs require approval of the window color, frame finish, and hardware style. Timeline: HOA approval can take 30–60 days, which is longer than the county permit process. Restrictions: Some HOAs restrict the visibility of the window frame from the exterior street view. You must submit “Spec Sheets” from the manufacturer to the HOA architectural review committee before signing a contract. Fine Risk: Failure to get HOA approval before installation can result in fines and orders to remove the windows at the homeowner’s expense. How to Save Money Bundle with Roof Replacement: If your roof is nearing the end of its life, install impact windows simultaneously. Many contractors offer a 5–10% discount on labor when bundling roofing and window projects. Take Advantage of Insurance Credits: In 2026, Florida insurers offer wind mitigation credits for impact windows. This can lower your annual premium by 20–40%, saving thousands over the life of the policy. Ask your insurer for a “Wind Mitigation Inspection” after installation. Choose Standard Sizes: Avoid custom shapes. Using standard grid sizes allows the manufacturer to use existing molds rather than creating new ones, reducing the unit cost by 15–20%. Utilize My Safe Florida Home Program: Check eligibility for the My Safe Florida Home program, which offers grants up to $10,000 for wind mitigation improvements, including impact windows, for eligible homeowners. Get multiple quotes: Florida law requires licensed contractors. Use myfloridalicense.com to verify. Getting 3+ quotes typically saves 10–20%. Warning Signs to Watch For ⚠️ These are red flags when hiring a contractor in Florida.
Unlicensed Operators: Any contractor offering to do work without a valid Florida license is operating illegally. Verify their license number online. No Permit Pulling: If a contractor says, “We don’t need a permit,” they are likely cutting corners. This leaves you liable for code violations. Cash-Only Deals: Contractors demanding cash payments without a contract are often avoiding taxes and insurance. This also leaves you with no legal recourse if the work is defective. Upfront Payment Demands: Florida law prohibits taking more than 10% or $1,000 upfront for home improvement contracts. If they ask for 50% down, walk away. Storm Chaser Scams: After hurricanes, out-of-state contractors flood Florida. Verify any contractor’s Florida license at myfloridalicense.com before signing anything.
How to Hire a Contractor in Florida Step 1: Verify the License All Florida contractors must hold a state license. Check at myfloridalicense.com. For impact window work, look for a Certified General Contractor (CGC) license. Some specialized window companies may hold a Certified Asbestos General (CAG) license if they handle old framing, but a CGC is the standard for structural window replacement. Do not hire a handyman for impact windows; it requires structural engineering knowledge.
Step 2: Get the Permit In Florida, most home improvement projects require a permit. Your contractor should pull it — if they say you don’t need one, that’s a red flag. Permit fees typically run $500–$2,500 depending on county. In Palm Beach, the contractor must submit the Florida Product Approval (FPA) number for the specific window model being installed.
Step 3: Get 3+ Quotes Prices vary 20–40% between contractors. Get quotes from at least three. Each quote should include:
Brand and model of windows. Glass type (Low-E, Argon). Frame material and color. Detailed scope of work (including flashing and sealant). Warranty terms (manufacturer vs. installer). Permit and inspection fees. Step 4: Check Insurance Contractor must carry general liability AND workers’ comp. Ask for certificates of insurance, not just verbal confirmation. If a worker gets hurt on your property and the contractor lacks insurance, you could be sued.
Step 5: Understand Your Contract Florida Statute 713.01 requires a written contract for any home improvement over $1,000. The contract must include:
Total price and payment schedule. Start and completion dates. Warranty details. Payment Schedule: Never pay more than 10% or $1,000 upfront per Florida law. Do not make final payment until the county issues a final inspection approval. FAQ How long does window installation take in Florida? For a typical 20-window home, installation takes 3–5 days. However, the timeline is often delayed by HOA approval (30 days) and permit review (14 days). Manufacturing lead times in 2026 average 8–12 weeks for custom orders.
Do I need a permit for window installation in Florida? Yes. In Palm Beach County, replacing windows is a permit-required activity because it affects the building envelope. You must submit product approval numbers and plans to the building department.
Can I do this myself in Florida? No. Florida law requires a licensed contractor for structural window installation. DIY installation can void the window warranty and is a violation of the Florida Building Code.
What’s the best time of year to install windows in Florida? The best time is late fall or winter (November–February). This avoids hurricane season (June–November) and the peak summer heat, which can affect sealant curing. Contractor availability is also higher during these months.
Will this affect my homeowner’s insurance in Florida? Yes, positively. Installing impact windows is the #1 way to lower windstorm premiums in Florida. You can save 20–40% on your premium, which can pay for the windows over time. Notify your insurer immediately after installation.
How do I find a licensed impact window contractor in Florida? Use myfloridalicense.com to search by license type (CGC) and status (Active). Look for contractors with at least 5 years of experience specifically in impact glazing. Check the Better Business Bureau (BBB) for complaints.
Bottom Line Installing impact windows in Palm Beach in 2026 is a significant investment ranging from $5,000 to $45,000, but it is essential for safety and insurance savings.
Palm Beach homeowners should budget $5,000–$45,000 for this project in 2026. The biggest cost drivers are the wind zone requirements, frame material choice, and permit fees. To get the best price: verify your contractor’s Florida license (CGC), pull the permit yourself if needed, and get at least 3 quotes before signing anything.
